description: Systematic performance analysis for GPU kernels: the roofline model, arithmetic intensity, Nsight Compute workflow, warp efficiency metrics, and Nsight Systems for multi-kernel concurrency analysis.

concepts.title: Roofline Model
concepts.preciseDefinition: A visual performance model relating a kernel's arithmetic intensity (FLOPs/byte) to achievable performance (FLOPs/s). Bounded above by two roofs: (1) the memory bandwidth roof (slope × arithmetic intensity) and (2) the arithmetic bandwidth roof (flat ceiling). A kernel's performance point is the minimum of the two. The ridge point is where the two roofs meet: ridge = arithmetic_bandwidth / memory_bandwidth (FLOPs/byte).
keyFacts: H100 ridge point (BF16 TC): 989 TFLOPS / 3.35 TB/s = 295 FLOPs/byte
A100 ridge point (BF16): 312 / 2.0 = 156 FLOPs/byte
SAXPY: 1 FMA / 12 bytes = 0.083 FLOPs/byte — 3500× below H100 ridge → deeply memory-bound
Matrix multiply NxN: N/6 FLOPs/byte → compute-bound on H100 for N > 1770
Attention mechanism: O(1/seq_len) arithmetic intensity — memory-bound for long sequences (motivates FlashAttention)
commonMisconceptions: A kernel at the roofline is optimal — it is at the theoretical maximum; actual kernels have additional overheads
Roofline uses wall-clock time for bandwidth — bandwidth is measured as bytes actually transferred, not allocated
There is only one roofline — there are separate rooflines for HBM bandwidth, L2 bandwidth, Tensor Core bandwidth

concepts.title: Arithmetic Intensity
concepts.preciseDefinition: FLOPs performed per byte of memory moved between compute units and DRAM. Determines where a kernel sits on the roofline model. Computed as: arithmetic_intensity = (FLOPs) / (bytes_read + bytes_written). Higher is better for compute efficiency. The key insight: matrix multiplication has O(N) arithmetic intensity while elementwise operations have O(1), making GEMM fundamentally more efficient at GPU utilization.
keyFacts: SAXPY: (2N FLOPs) / (12N bytes) = 1/6 FLOPs/byte
FFT: 5/2 N log(N) FLOPs / 16N bytes = 5/32 log(N) FLOPs/byte — O(log N) scaling
SGEMM: 2N³ / (4N² × 3) = N/6 FLOPs/byte — O(N) scaling
Gradient checkpointing increases arithmetic intensity by trading recomputation for reduced memory traffic
Operation fusion increases arithmetic intensity by eliminating intermediate writes to global memory
commonMisconceptions: Arithmetic intensity is about the algorithm's asymptotic complexity — it is an absolute ratio for a specific problem size
Higher arithmetic intensity always means faster execution — only up to the compute roof; then memory bandwidth is not the bottleneck
Reducing memory traffic always improves performance — only if memory-bound; compute-bound kernels are not helped by less memory traffic

concepts.title: Nsight Compute
concepts.preciseDefinition: NVIDIA's per-kernel GPU performance profiler. Collects hardware counter metrics through multiple kernel replay passes, providing: roofline positioning, memory throughput (L1/L2/HBM), warp efficiency, compute throughput, stall reasons, and source-to-SASS correlation. The guided optimization workflow uses automated 'speedup bottleneck' analysis to prioritize optimization efforts.
keyFacts: CLI: ncu --set full --export report.ncu-rep -o report kernel_binary
Key metrics: sm__throughput.avg.pct_of_peak_sustained_elapsed (SM active time), l1tex__t_bytes_pipe_lsu_mem_global_op_ld.sum (L1 global load bytes)
Roofline section: positions kernel on arithmetic bandwidth vs memory bandwidth plot
Warp State Statistics section: shows fraction of cycles in each stall reason
Source correlation: maps SASS instructions back to CUDA C++ source lines
Replay: multiple kernel invocations to collect all counter groups; introduces ~3-10× slowdown
commonMisconceptions: Nsight Compute measures exact wall-clock time — replay overhead makes timing inaccurate; use CUDA events for timing
All metrics can be collected in one pass — hardware counter limitations require multiple replay passes
High SM active time means the kernel is optimal — SM can be active but underutilizing compute (stall-heavy)

concepts.title: Warp Efficiency Metrics
concepts.preciseDefinition: A set of Nsight Compute metrics that characterize how efficiently warps use SM resources. Key metrics: (1) Warp Occupancy = active_warps / max_warps, (2) Issue Efficiency = issued_ipc / peak_ipc, (3) Branch Efficiency = non_divergent_branches / total_branches, (4) Global Load Efficiency = requested_bytes / actual_transactions × 128, (5) Shared Memory Bank Conflicts per access.
keyFacts: Issue Efficiency of 100% = every cycle had an eligible warp (latency fully hidden)
Global Memory Load Efficiency <100% = non-coalesced accesses (look for strided access patterns)
Long Scoreboard stalls = memory-bound (increase occupancy or use prefetch)
Pipe utilization (TEX, LSU, ADU pipes) reveals which functional units are saturated
Warp cycles per issued instruction = average instruction latency in cycles
commonMisconceptions: High warp occupancy guarantees high performance — occupancy helps latency hiding but doesn't help compute-bound kernels
100% global load efficiency means coalesced access — also requires no cache misses (repeated loads from L1/L2 are efficient)
All stall types are equally bad — compute-bound kernels with high ILP can tolerate high stall fractions

exercises.title: Profile and Optimize a Kernel with Nsight Compute
exercises.whatItTeaches: End-to-end Nsight Compute profiling workflow on a realistic kernel (e.g., reduction or GEMM); interpreting roofline position; identifying top bottleneck; applying fix; measuring improvement.
exercises.starterCodeHint: // Profile with: ncu --set full ./program; // Look at: Memory Workload Analysis, Compute Workload Analysis, Warp State Statistics
exercises.testCaseHint: Start with a non-coalesced access kernel; profile to find low Global Load Efficiency; fix by transposing access pattern; re-profile to confirm improvement

concepts.title: Nsight Systems
concepts.preciseDefinition: A system-wide performance analysis tool that traces GPU kernel execution, memory transfers, CPU thread activity, and CUDA API calls on a unified timeline. Distinguishes from Nsight Compute in that it shows multi-kernel concurrency, stream overlap, and CPU-GPU synchronization overhead — not per-kernel hardware counter metrics.
keyFacts: CLI: nsys profile --stats=true ./program; outputs .nsys-rep file
Shows timeline of: CUDA kernels, cudaMemcpy, CPU functions, NVTX ranges
NVTX annotations: nvtxRangePushA/nvtxRangePop to label code regions on timeline
Key patterns to spot: unnecessary synchronization bubbles, underutilized GPU between kernel launches, H2D/D2H blocking compute
Nsight Systems (system-level timeline) + Nsight Compute (per-kernel counters) = complete profiling workflow
commonMisconceptions: Nsight Compute and Nsight Systems do the same thing — they are complementary; Systems for concurrency, Compute for per-kernel
cudaDeviceSynchronize() is always needed for profiling — only needed when measuring wall time on CPU; avoid in production
NVTX ranges are free — they have small overhead (~microsecond per annotation)

concepts.title: Identifying and Addressing Bottlenecks
concepts.preciseDefinition: A kernel is memory-bound if its runtime scales with memory_bytes / memory_bandwidth, and compute-bound if it scales with flops / arithmetic_bandwidth. Common optimization strategies: for memory-bound → increase arithmetic intensity (fusion, tiling, quantization); for compute-bound → reduce register pressure, improve ILP, upgrade to Tensor Cores. The roofline model determines which bound applies and how far below the roof the kernel sits.
keyFacts: Memory-bound optimizations: coalescing, shared memory reuse, tiling, reducing redundant loads, quantization
Compute-bound optimizations: ILP, unrolling, Tensor Core usage, register blocking, dual-issue
Fusion: merging multiple memory-bound kernels eliminates intermediate reads/writes — most impactful optimization for LLM inference
Prefetching with async copies (ldgsts, cp.async) can overlap computation and memory for some patterns
Bandwidth utilization = achieved_bandwidth / peak_bandwidth; target >80% for memory-bound kernels
commonMisconceptions: One can be both memory-bound and compute-bound simultaneously — a kernel hits only one roof at a time
Kernel fusion always helps — fusion can increase register pressure and reduce occupancy if not done carefully
Fixing memory access pattern always speeds up a compute-bound kernel — memory optimizations have no impact when compute is the bottleneck

coverageGaps: CUPTI-based custom profiling — reading raw hardware counters from user code; advanced use case, not planned
Power and thermal profiling (nvidia-smi, NVML) — relevant for production deployment but not covered in curriculum
Compute Sanitizer for race condition detection — important for debugging, not covered
